title: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: control sample rotation
exclusions: Does NOT perform the audit; attests that the internal-audit programme has exercised at least N% of ISMS controls over the rolling year and that no control was audited twice while another was never audited.
evidence: Saved search 'iso_27001_9_2_audit_rotation' (weekly) emits: control_id, last_audited, audit_count_365d, due_for_audit, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Clause 9.2 requires an internal-audit programme exercised at planned intervals; rotation evidence directly satisfies the planned-interval requirement.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 9.2 (Internal audit) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.50: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: control sample rotation.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.50' running on sourcetype archer:audit_test and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SOC 2 CC4.1 — monitoring effectiveness of controls, contributing.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC-2 CC4.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.50: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: control sample rotation.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.50' running on sourcetype archer:audit_test and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 CA-2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.50: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: control sample rotation.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.50' running on sourcetype archer:audit_test and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A catalog control with no audit_test events in 365d returns due_for_audit=1 in the weekly run.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A control audited within the window is absent from the overdue table.

dataSources: `index=grc` sourcetype=archer:audit_test OR sourcetype=servicenow:audit_finding; ISMS control catalogue `iso_27001_control_catalog.csv`. (Splunkbase 1621 — Splunk CIM Add-on)
spl:
| inputlookup iso_27001_control_catalog.csv
| join type=outer control_id
  [search index=grc sourcetype IN (archer:audit_test,servicenow:audit_finding) earliest=-365d
   | stats latest(_time) AS last_audited count AS audit_count_365d BY control_id]
| eval days_since=round((now()-last_audited)/86400,0)
| eval due_for_audit=if(isnull(last_audited) OR days_since>365,1,0)
| table control_id category last_audited days_since audit_count_365d due_for_audit
description: Joins the ISMS control catalogue with internal-audit test records over the trailing 365 days. The output tells the Audit Committee which controls are overdue.
value: Closes the typical gap where internal audit over-samples 'hot' controls and silently skips administrative ones. The rotation evidence is what ISO 9.2 requires and what the registrar looks for.
implementation: (1) Maintain iso_27001_control_catalog.csv; (2) require internal-audit output to index=grc with control_id tag; (3) schedule UC weekly; (4) due_for_audit=1 adds the control to the next quarter audit plan; (5) annual Audit Committee report. Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=archer:audit_test`, `sourcetype=servicenow:audit_finding`, `sourcetype=servicenow:audit_finding;`.
visualization: Stacked bar of audit count by category, table of overdue controls, single value 'controls never audited'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Assessment cycle alignment** — Control assessments following annual cycles may show brief non-current periods between assessment completion and report publication.

2. **Finding remediation within SLA** — Open findings within their documented remediation timeline are being addressed per policy, not neglected or overlooked.

3. **Framework version transitions** — Migration between control framework versions (ISO 27001:2013 to 2022) creates temporary mapping gaps during the documented transition period.

4. **Accepted residual risk items** — Findings formally accepted through the risk management governance process with documented compensating controls and management sign-off.

5. **Evidence collection cadence differences** — Controls with different evidence generation frequencies (real-time vs. quarterly) show varying currency in compliance dashboards.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `grc` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `iso_27001_control_catalog.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('iso_27001_control_catalog.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `9.2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO/IEC 27001 9.2 (Internal audit) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.50: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: control sample rotation." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `grc` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain iso_27001_control_catalog.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** require internal-audit output to index=grc with control_id tag. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC weekly. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** due_for_audit=1 adds the control to the next quarter audit plan. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** annual Audit Committee report. Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=archer:audit_test`, `sourcetype=servicenow:audit_finding`, `sourcetype=servicenow:audit_finding;`. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: control sample rotation," the aggregation measures control coverage — the output rows enumerate what IS covered, and an auditor's follow-on question ('what about the rest?') is answered by the gap between this result and the authoritative population.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`grc`, sourcetype=`the primary`) with a subsearch on `control_id` across the last year. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.50: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: con]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -365d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.50,reg=ISO/IEC_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `control_id`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table control_id, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_since`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_since) as p50 perc90(days_since) as p90 perc99(days_since) as p99 max(days_since) as hi
```

The current threshold `365` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.50: ISO/IEC 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Stacked Bar Of Audit Count By Category (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Overdue Controls (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Controls Never Audited' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO/IEC 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clause 9.2 — Internal audit coverage: control sample rotation. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
